video_title: The TRUTH About STRC, SATA, Strategy & Strive | Matt Cole
gist: Strive CEO Matt Cole explains the mechanics, carry-trade economics, and risk structure of Bitcoin-backed 'digital credit' (Strive's SATA preferred, Strategy's STRC/stretch), argues these perpetual preferreds are the key low-volatility bridge asset for the fiat-to-Bitcoin transition, defends Michael Saylor's first Bitcoin sale as a healthy capital-allocation evolution, and lays out the case for a multi-issuer digital-credit ecosystem scaling into 2027-2029.

insights.claim: Strive's SATA digital-credit instrument is a perpetual preferred-equity security backed by Bitcoin, currently paying a variable ~13% rate that the issuer pegs to keep the price near par ($100), versus ~11.5% on Strategy's stretch (STRC) products.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole (CEO Strive Asset Management)
insights.so_what: Sets the explicit yield benchmark for the new Bitcoin-backed preferred category; a ~13% perpetual yield reprices what a fixed-income allocator can earn versus HYG's ~6.5%.
insights.quote: for SATA, it's 13% right now. It's variable rate um because the issuer we are trying to peg it minimize volatility around 100

insights.claim: For the issuer, digital credit is a carry trade: Strive profits if Bitcoin's compounded annual growth rate exceeds the financing cost (~13% for SATA, ~11.5% for Strategy), making Bitcoin's CAGR the hurdle rate for common-equity holders to outperform Bitcoin.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Defines the exact condition under which the common equity (ASST/MSTR) beats spot Bitcoin: BTC CAGR must clear the preferred coupon.
insights.quote: what we are making a investment bet on is that the average compounded annual growth rate of bitcoin into the future will be better than that financing rate

insights.claim: The break-even Bitcoin appreciation for SATA to pay its coupon in perpetuity is only ~6-12% per year, far below the rate needed for common equity to win; at ~6.5% BTC growth the preferred is fully covered while the common equity likely stays flat as all Bitcoin returns filter into the preferred.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Quantifies the safety margin on the preferred and the trade-off: SATA holders win on modest BTC growth, but common equity needs BTC to clear the full coupon to outperform.
insights.quote: for SATA right now it's in the neighborhood of 6 12% as that break even interest rate where Strive could pay interest literally forever into the future

insights.claim: Digital credit is structured to eliminate two issuer risks: maturity risk (perpetual liability, no refinancing) and negative convexity (no equity conversion when Bitcoin rips higher), with the issuer willing to pay a double-digit coupon to remove both.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Explains why perpetual preferred beats convertible debt for a BTC treasury: it removes forced-seller maturity walls and caps no upside.
insights.quote: I was happy to pay a double-digit interest rate to not have the negative convexity to the downside of maturity risk and not have negative convexity to the upside of equity conversion

insights.claim: The single irreducible risk borne by SATA preferred holders is maturity risk, which the issuer deliberately offloaded to investors in exchange for the high coupon; all other listed risks should be probability-weighted by the buyer.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Frames the real due-diligence question for a SATA buyer as: what is the probability of a Bitcoin scenario severe enough to force a dividend pause.
insights.quote: there's really one true risk to these instruments... that key risk was maturity risk

insights.claim: Strive carries no debt and holds ~18 months of cash and marketable securities (over 12 months cash plus ~6 months of stretch/STRC reserves) as a dividend buffer for SATA.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Concrete balance-sheet cushion figure lets analysts model how long SATA dividends survive a BTC drawdown without selling Bitcoin.
insights.quote: Strive has no debt. uh we have 18 months of cash and marketable securities

insights.claim: If a 2022-2023-style bear market repeated (implying a ~$40,000 Bitcoin bottom with no recovery off the 200-week MA until late 2027), Strive could pay SATA dividends entirely from cash reserves without selling a single Bitcoin.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Stress-test scenario: at $40k BTC, the reserve alone covers a multi-year bear, so dividend-pause risk only appears in a worse-than-2022 drawdown.
insights.quote: what that would mean in Bitcoin terms today would be about a $40,000 Bitcoin bottom

insights.claim: Beyond cash reserves, Strive estimates 5-7 additional years of dividend coverage by selling Bitcoin (depending on BTC price), so a dividend pause only becomes plausible in a bear market substantially longer and deeper than 2022-2023.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Sizes total dividend runway (reserves + BTC sales) at well over a market cycle, pushing the dividend-cut tail risk far out.
insights.quote: $40,000, 5 to seven years of Bitcoin coverage in of itself

insights.claim: Strive increased its SATA dividend reserve from 12 months to 18 months during the current Bitcoin bear market despite no obligation to, and despite SATA having been IPO'd in November when Bitcoin was above $100,000.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Actions-over-words signal: the issuer is building, not drawing down, its buffer into weakness, which supports confidence in the par peg.
insights.quote: what Strive has actually done is we've increased the dividend reserve from 12 months to 18 months

insights.claim: Strive's board has a fiduciary obligation to pause SATA dividends only if paying would risk bankruptcy; the issuer can also lower the variable rate, giving multiple levers before any default.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Defines the legal trigger and flexibility of the instrument: dividends can be cut or rate-reduced, so it is not a hard contractual default like debt.
insights.quote: we could pause dividends and we actually would have a a fiduciary obligation as a board to pause dividends if paying a dividend would put Strive into bankruptcy risk

insights.claim: The fraction of each SATA dollar raised that goes into Bitcoin versus the dividend reserve is dynamic; Strive sometimes deploys 100% into Bitcoin and is willing to dip into reserves if liquidity conditions dry up, prioritizing confidence in the par peg over a fixed reserve ratio.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Reserve policy is discretionary, not contractual, so SATA's BTC-backing ratio varies with market conditions and issuance pace.

insights.claim: Strive's long-term Bitcoin projection is ~30% CAGR over the next couple of decades, and the projected CAGR rises during bear markets to compensate for the drawdown, putting current expectations at the higher end of the range.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: A ~30% BTC CAGR assumption underpins the entire carry-trade thesis; it is the input that makes SATA's ~13% coupon look comfortably covered.
insights.quote: on average call it a 30% kagger is what I think uh we should expect

insights.claim: For a clean-balance-sheet Bitcoin treasury company, Strive views ~30-70% amplification (leverage) as the manageable range, intending to stop near the upper bound when Bitcoin sits around its 200-week moving average, and advocates pushing leverage aggressively at current depressed prices.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Quantifies the leverage band BTC treasury firms target and flags that issuers may add the most leverage near cycle lows ('greedy when others are fearful').
insights.quote: somewhere you know call it in the neighborhood of 30 to 60 70% is kind of for a clean balance sheet company the range of amplification

insights.claim: Cole is bullish on Bitcoin for the back half of the current year, calling a $40,000 bottom a more-bearish-than-base-case scenario rather than his expectation.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: The issuer's own base case is a 2H recovery, not the $40k stress case used for reserve sizing.
insights.quote: I'm pretty bullish on Bitcoin for the back half of this year

insights.claim: Strive's SATA will be the first listed US security to pay a daily dividend (on business days), versus Strategy's stretch moving to twice-monthly and earlier monthly payouts, deliberately to smooth volatility so the instrument behaves more like a money-market fund or savings account.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Daily dividends are the structural lever to compress price volatility around par and reposition SATA as a cash-substitute, expanding its addressable market.
insights.quote: it will be the first, literally the first listed security in US capital markets history. So like real innovation here to pay a daily dividend

insights.claim: Monthly-dividend digital credit shows a recurring pattern: price rises toward par with a sharp liquidity/volume spike just before the dividend event, then drops roughly by the dividend amount afterward, making volume (not price) the key tell of underlying behavior.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Actionable trading pattern: the pre-dividend volume spike and post-dividend par drop is exploitable/observable in STRC and SATA on monthly schedules.
insights.quote: you'll see the price of stretch or SATA move up to par right around the dividend event and you'll see the volume spike

insights.claim: Cole expects digital credit to be tokenized, enabling near-instantaneous dividends and consumer financial products (debit/credit cards, banking features) layered on top, ultimately turning Bitcoin-backed preferreds into a spendable currency alternative.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Points to tokenized securities + card rails as the next product layer, a roadmap for fintech/tokenization plays adjacent to BTC treasuries.
insights.quote: I think there's no better security to tokenize than digital credit

themes.theme: Saylor's Bitcoin sale & Strategy narrative risk

insights.claim: Cole reads Michael Saylor's sale of 32 Bitcoin as a necessary, healthy evolution that ends Strategy's absolutist 'never sell' messaging and signals capacity to manage the balance sheet for total return, including potential tax-loss-harvesting sales with immediate buy-backs.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Counters the bearish 'tip of the iceberg' narrative: the sale reduces forced-seller tail risk and is bullish for MSTR's risk profile despite consensus reading it as capitulation.
insights.quote: I think it was a a necessary evolution from strategy

insights.claim: The fact that a mere 32-Bitcoin sale shocked the market proves to Cole that Saylor had to break the 'never sell' precedent now to gain operating flexibility, and he expects Strategy to be a net Bitcoin buyer effectively every month going forward.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: If Strategy is a net monthly BTC buyer, the 'continual selling' narrative ages poorly; watch monthly net BTC flow as the falsification test.
insights.quote: I think he's going to be a net buyer of Bitcoin effectively every single month into the future

insights.claim: Strive explicitly told institutional investors during the SATA IPO process that it will sell Bitcoin when advantageous, and institutions appreciated that stance, contrasting with Strategy's prior 'never sell' posture.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Signals that sophisticated institutional buyers prefer flexible BTC-treasury mandates over dogmatic HODL, a differentiator for Strive.
insights.quote: when we launched SATA in our investor calls the IPO process we explicitly said that if we need to sell Bitcoin we will sell Bitcoin

insights.claim: Cole concedes the shift from 'never sell' to 'intelligent capital allocator, sometimes buyer sometimes seller' makes Strategy's story more nuanced and harder for retail to grasp, creating near-term narrative/perception risk even if it is the correct capital-allocation decision.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Identifies a genuine sentiment overhang on MSTR: a more sophisticated, less sloganized story can weaken the simple retail bull narrative short-term.

insights.claim: Since issuance, both SATA and Strategy's stretch (STRC) have delivered positive total returns even as Bitcoin fell ~50% from its all-time high, because their high yields more than offset modest price drops below par, demonstrating very low correlation to Bitcoin.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: The proof-point institutions demanded: digital credit held up in a 50% BTC drawdown, validating it as a low-volatility, low-correlation income sleeve.
insights.quote: the total return for both SATA and Stretch since they've been issued is positive still because they're paying a high yield

insights.claim: Cole pitches digital credit against the HYG high-yield ETF: HYG yields ~6.5% to worst with high volatility, whereas digital credit roughly doubles that yield with lower volatility, making it structurally attractive to institutions accustomed to high-yield risk.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Direct relative-value comparison: ~13% yield with sub-HYG volatility is the institutional sales pitch and a benchmark to watch versus HYG spreads.
insights.quote: if you just pull up a chart of the HYG high yield ETF and you look at how volatile that thing is and that thing has a yield to worst of 6 12% or so and you double that with digital credit

insights.claim: Cole expects mass institutional adoption of digital credit and Bitcoin ETFs to scale through 2027-2029, framing both ETFs and digital credit (which has existed under a year) as still early in a 3-5 year institutional track-record-building process.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Sets a multi-year adoption timeline; the 2027-2029 window is when investment-policy-statement approvals and track records unlock larger flows.
insights.quote: you could see mass institutional adoption continue to scale in 2027 2028 2029

insights.claim: Current digital-credit demand is being funded primarily out of fixed-income sleeves (the '40' of the 60/40), with notable flows from real-estate investors selling property to capture more yield with less work.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Identifies the capital sources being cannibalized: bond allocations and hard-asset/real-estate holdings, useful for sizing the displacement opportunity.
insights.quote: we've seen a lot of examples of real estate investors actually selling some real estate property and buying digital credit

insights.claim: Strive publishes the live risk profile of SATA and its common equity ASST on its website, refreshing every 15 seconds, contrasting with private credit's quarterly opaque marks.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Real-time risk transparency is a differentiator versus private credit and a tool allocators can monitor directly at strive.com.
insights.quote: you can go to our website and the risk of SATA and the risk of AST, it refreshes every 15 seconds

insights.claim: Cole argues the opportunity set for Bitcoin-backed credit is 'close to infinite' and that no single issuer (including Strive) can or should offer all products, citing obvious adjacencies like SATA-equivalents denominated in pounds, yen, and euros for international demand.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Flags currency-denominated BTC-backed preferreds as a near-term international product whitespace and a reason to expect many new issuers.
insights.quote: SATA but paid in the pound. Seda but paid in the yen. Seda but paid in the euro

insights.claim: Cole frames competition between Strive and Strategy as a positive-sum 'friendly' rivalry that drives innovation (cash reserves, willingness to sell BTC, daily dividends) and grows the total pie, while conceding it can shift capital flows from one issuer to the other in the short term.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Suggests issuer competition is a feature accelerating product quality, but near-term flow share between STRC and SATA can swing as features leapfrog.
insights.quote: the great thing about capitalism is that that competition pushes innovation

insights.claim: Strive worked NASDAQ and DTCC to approve the first daily-dividend security, an innovation now effectively open-source for competitors to copy, after Strategy had earlier (incorrectly) assumed NASDAQ rules capped dividend dollar amounts.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Regulatory/market-plumbing detail: daily dividends required NASDAQ + DTCC sign-off, a precedent that lowers the bar for all future issuers.
insights.quote: we found a way within the system to get NASDAQ and DTCC to say yes

insights.claim: Cole contends a diversified ecosystem of 20-50-100 digital-credit issuers reduces systemic risk and single-points-of-failure (including US-government attack surface), and notes a compliant 1940-Act digital-credit ETF would require at least ~30 issuers to be diversified.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: A digital-credit ETF (a major future demand channel) is gated on having ~30 issuers, so issuer proliferation is a precondition watch-item.
insights.quote: if you wanted a digital credit ETF, that you need probably at least 30 issuers of digital credit to have a diversified compliant 1940 act product

insights.claim: Cole expects interest-bearing tokenized securities and digital credit to threaten banks by enabling deposit flight, citing the Jamie Dimon vs Brian Armstrong fight over interest-bearing stablecoins as evidence banks see a systemic run risk.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Frames banks as the 'Blockbuster' incumbents at risk from yield-bearing crypto instruments, a long-term structural short/disruption thesis on bank deposits.
insights.quote: Think about Jamie Diamond, how he's losing his um mind about stable coins paying interest

insights.claim: Cole projects Michael Saylor's Strategy reaches ~1,000,000 Bitcoin (≈5% of supply) likely this year, and argues 5% is a material but non-controlling stake that does not threaten the network even if doubled.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Sizes the largest corporate BTC holder's likely 2026 position at ~5% of supply, relevant for concentration-risk and supply-overhang debates.
insights.quote: when I think about Sailor's stack he probably gets to a million Bitcoin I think probably this year

insights.claim: Cole holds Bitcoin and amplified (leveraged) Bitcoin exposure rather than digital credit himself, stating digital credit is designed for volatility-averse investors who need cash flow, while high-conviction holders should go further out the risk spectrum.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Issuer's own positioning is a candid tell: spot/leveraged BTC for conviction holders, SATA for income/volatility-averse buyers, segmenting the market.
insights.quote: I do not own digital credit. I own Bitcoin and I own amplified Bitcoin exposure because I'm so confident in the direction that this goes

insights.claim: Cole argues only a tiny self-custodied Bitcoin position is needed for insurance, citing giving family members 0.05 BTC each in 2017 as 'more than the average American could hold' even if 100% of Bitcoin were held in America.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Reframes the concentration objection: because BTC is scarce, modest self-custody suffices, so institutional/ETF/treasury custody of most supply is acceptable.
insights.quote: with 0.05 Bitcoin, even if 100% of the Bitcoin was held in America, you will have more Bitcoin than the average American can hold

insights.claim: Cole's core macro thesis is that the US debt crisis will not improve and the economy is transitioning to a Bitcoin future, with digital credit positioned as potentially the most important low-volatility bridge asset that could itself accelerate hyperbitcoinization by drawing fresh demand.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: The macro frame underpinning every product claim: digital credit is sold as the smoothed on-ramp for capital fleeing fiat, with a self-reinforcing demand loop into BTC.
insights.quote: I think digital credit could be the most important asset in this transition period

insights.claim: Cole treats the '60/40 portfolio is dead' as consensus within crypto circles but argues the unresolved question is what replaces the 40% (Bitcoin, prediction markets, trend-following, or digital credit), positioning digital credit as a contender for that allocation.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Identifies the contested fixed-income replacement decision as the battleground digital credit is competing to win, alongside other alt-fixed-income solutions.
insights.quote: what is completely not consensus is what do you do with the 40

insights.claim: Cole argues regulation cannot ultimately constrain digital credit's growth because 'the problem is the money, the dollar,' and expects securities broadly to become dollar alternatives as consumers exit fiat via tokenized, card-linked instruments.
insights.speaker: guest, Matt Cole
insights.so_what: Long-horizon view that yield-bearing tokenized securities displace dollars regardless of regulatory pushback, a structural tailwind for the category.
insights.quote: you can't constrain through regulation where this will ultimately go
